title: Braised red cabbage
subtitle: Slow-cooked with apple, vinegar, and clove
excerpt: Red cabbage braised low and long with Bramley apple, red wine vinegar, and a few cloves until it collapses into something glossy, deeply purple, and sweet-sharp. Better the next day, which makes it the ideal Christmas and Sunday-roast side.
sourceNotes: Classic British and northern European braise documented in various forms in Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, and in Elizabeth David's English Food (1977), drawn from historical English and German braising traditions.

content.text: Red cabbage braised with apple and vinegar is the side dish that can be prepared days in advance and only improves. An hour and a half in a low oven reduces the sharp raw cabbage to something almost jammy: deep-coloured, slightly sweet, and with enough acidity to cut through the fat of goose, duck, or pork. It takes 20 minutes of preparation and then the oven does the rest.

The vinegar is essential: it keeps the cabbage a vivid purple throughout the long cook. Without it, the pigments shift towards a dull blue-grey. Red wine vinegar or cider vinegar both work well; the amount below gives a balanced result but can be adjusted at the end.

items.name: Red cabbage
items.prepNote: about 700 g, quartered, cored, and thinly sliced

content.text: Preheat the oven to 150°C fan. Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of unsalted butter in a lidded casserole dish over medium heat. Add {{onion}} thinly sliced onion and cook for 3 minutes until softened. Add the sliced cabbage, {{apple-bramley}} roughly chopped apple, the cloves, and the bay leaves (and cinnamon if using). Pour over {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar and scatter {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar over the top.

Stir everything together, cover tightly, and transfer to the oven. Braise for 1 hour 30 minutes, stirring once or twice during cooking. The cabbage should be very tender, glossy, and deeply coloured. If liquid remains in the base of the pot, remove the lid and return to the oven for 10 minutes to reduce it. Remove the bay leaves and whole cloves before serving. Season with salt and a splash more vinegar if it needs brightness.

items.fix: Stir in a tablespoon of red wine vinegar and cook for 5 more minutes. The acid shifts the colour back towards purple.
items.cause: Not enough acid during the cook

items.fix: Add a little more dark brown sugar, a teaspoon at a time, and stir over low heat.
items.cause: Vinegar proportion is high or the apple was very tart

items.fix: Add a splash of water and continue with the lid on. Check more frequently.
items.cause: Lid was not tight enough or the oven was hotter than expected
items.symptom: Liquid has dried out during the braise

items.fix: Continue braising, covered, for another 20 to 30 minutes.
items.cause: Sliced too thickly, or the oven temperature was too low
items.symptom: Cabbage still has texture after 90 minutes

content.text: This is one of the rare dishes where making it 2 days in advance is actively recommended. The flavours deepen and mellow considerably; what is slightly sharp on the day becomes rounded and complex by the second day. Reheat in the covered casserole over low heat with 2 tablespoons of water, stirring occasionally, until hot through.

Braised red cabbage came to the British kitchen from German and Alsatian cooking. Both use the same apple, vinegar, and spice braise. The Germans call it Rotkohl, the French chou rouge braise. It was a fixed British winter side by the Victorian period, served with game and pork roasts. It belongs especially at Christmas, where the make-ahead reliability is as useful as the flavour.
